column242: Excellent approach to lingering neuroplastic pain. Self-knowledge and the willingness to confront the source and resolution of the migrating pain is potentially life-altering.    An area of investigation/evaluation is how to integrate the classical physician approach to pain relief and healing into the overall pain and wellness strategy, with PRT a core element.  We all go back to our PCP and are offered the 'standard' remedies.  How do we balance and prioritize our care, and specifically take an active role in its management?    For example, my spouse, and RN, is not sanguine on the PRT idea.  Sad as I  do find it very valuable.  I will not get support at home for the concept.  Fine, but that might be an issue for others.  Our partners are often steeped in the classical medical approaches of "it is broke, either drugs, cold steel, or PT".   Been there done that, and I am trying to work the other approaches, including PRT that have a bearing on my problems.  An example was weight loss.  I lost weight when we followed the South Beach diet together.  That ended up as too much work and insufficient for the family members that live with us. I gained virtually all of it back over the past 20 years (actually got it back faster but plateaued since then).  Question might be, what help might you have at home to help cement these techniques, or other resources as partners in this technique.

column242: I would have like longer or a few more sessions. I felt like the time was a little too short. I felt like some of the sessions felt cut off and we didn't get all we could have out of the sessions.    I also would have like maybe a few in person sessions, being online means that there are going to be distractions even when you try to minimize or get rid of them. I think having maybe the first somatic tracking session in person would be beneficial as well as one or 2 other sessions.    I think you should ask if you've had medical professionals say that the pain is all in your head. Women especially are told that pain is caused by mental illnesses instead of physical. it was great that you said that the pain was real but sometimes doctors will dismiss patients when there is no structural issues found which can lead to feelings of distrust and more pain.

column242: I did PRT workshop once before in Jan 2024.  This workshop was much better.  i think that John & Rachel were much more relatable.    They worked very well together.  Seeing they both live with "chronic pain," they could share personal experiences.  I don't think the facilitator in the Jan was.  if she was, I don't recall her mentioning it.    This group had about half as many participants. Which was so much more helpful. I know the plan is to get as many veterans help.    Please keep the groups small, I was much more engaged as their were fewer of us and I had more opportunities to share my issues/experiences.  With that Jan group, we would have to stop sharing so that we could push forward & get "the lesson" done in a hour.  I think if there was a Veteran who was a PRT facilitator could be helpful.  Not required could be helpful.  I probably received more from this workshop comparedtothe1st one. Could be because that materials were repeated or due to the smaller group.  Another thing that John & Rachel did is have a lot of visual aides & videos.  For me, those helped "drive the point home."  I hope this program helps other Veteran's as much it has me.
